worth_it_explanation: Buyer and advertiser controls decide which demand FreeWheel accepts against a publisher's video and Connected TV (CTV) inventory, so they sit directly between brand safety and fill. A blocklist that has grown unchecked can suppress legitimate demand and the revenue that came with it, while an allowlist that was set once and forgotten can let unwanted advertisers through. The check is a review of the buyer and advertiser controls a publisher already has in FreeWheel, so it costs reading time rather than rebuild time, and the finding points to a concrete control the publisher can adjust and remeasure.
intro: Demand tag controls in FreeWheel are the allowlists and blocklists a publisher uses to decide which buyers and advertisers can transact against its video and CTV inventory. Programmatic demand reaches a FreeWheel publisher through real-time bidding, and the controls that shape it draw on the same standards-based fields that govern advertiser blocking across the wider market, such as the OpenRTB blocked-advertiser-domain and blocked-category lists. This subcategory is about whether those controls are maintained on purpose. The checks confirm that allowlists and blocklists reflect a current decision rather than an old setting left in place, and that the blocks in force are not so broad that they cut off legitimate demand and the fill that comes with it.

audit_checks.title: Buyer and advertiser allowlists and blocklists are not maintained intentionally
audit_checks.impact_rationale: Allowlists and blocklists that are set once and left untouched drift away from what the publisher actually wants. A stale blocklist keeps out demand that is no longer a concern, and a stale allowlist can keep admitting buyers the publisher would now exclude. Either way the controls stop reflecting current intent, so the publisher cannot say with confidence which demand it is accepting and why.

educate.base:
FreeWheel gives a publisher more than one way to decide which demand reaches its video and Connected TV (CTV) inventory. A blocklist names the advertisers, advertiser categories, or buyers the publisher will not accept, and the ad server rejects bids that match. An allowlist works the other way, naming the buyers or advertisers the publisher will accept and excluding everything else by default. Both are standard supply-side controls, and on the programmatic side they lean on the same fields the wider market uses, such as the OpenRTB blocked-advertiser-domain list and the blocked-category list that let a seller reject creative from named domains or content categories.

The audit question is not whether these lists exist. It is whether they are maintained on purpose. A blocklist grows over time as a publisher reacts to a bad creative, a complaint, or a one-off incident, and entries added that way can go without a second look. An allowlist set during an initial FreeWheel onboarding can stay frozen while the publisher's demand relationships change around it. In both cases the control keeps doing what it was last told to do, which may no longer match what the publisher wants.

Maintaining these lists intentionally means each entry has a reason on record and a point at which it gets reviewed. A blocked advertiser domain should trace back to a decision the publisher can still explain. An allowlisted buyer should still be one the publisher means to transact with. When the reasons are written down, the publisher can tell a current control from a leftover one and prune the list with confidence rather than leaving everything in place because no one remembers why it is there.

A few signs point to lists that are not maintained intentionally:
  • Blocklist or allowlist entries have no recorded reason, owner, or review date
  • The publisher cannot say why a given advertiser domain or category is blocked
  • The lists have not been reviewed since the FreeWheel inventory was first set up
You may want to confirm that each allowlist and blocklist entry still reflects a current decision. One approach is to attach a reason and a review date to every entry, then schedule a periodic pass that removes the ones that no longer apply.

site_type_overlays.publishing: On a streaming or broadcaster property, blocklists often carry advertiser exclusions tied to editorial standards, competitive separation, or a past sponsorship conflict. You may want to review those entries against current programming and sponsorship deals because a competitor block that made sense for last season's lineup can suppress legitimate demand once that arrangement ends.
site_type_overlays.ecommerce: On a commerce-content video property, allowlists and blocklists can reflect brand-suitability rules for the retailers and product categories shown alongside the content. You may want to confirm those entries still match current merchandising relationships because a category block set for one campaign can keep out demand the publisher would now welcome.

educate.learn_more_note: FreeWheel's detailed configuration documentation for buyer and advertiser allowlists and blocklists is in the partner portal (login required).

investigate.intro: Review the allowlists and blocklists applied to the publisher's FreeWheel inventory, and for each entry look for a recorded reason, an owner, and a review date.

freewheel_ui.label: FreeWheel console
steps: Open the buyer and advertiser controls for the publisher's inventory and list every allowlist and blocklist in force.
For each list, record the advertiser domains, advertiser categories, or buyers it names and whether it admits or rejects them.
For each entry, note whether a reason, an owner, and a review date are recorded, or whether the entry stands without any explanation.
Ask the publisher when the lists were last reviewed and record the date along with what changed in that review.

freewheel_reporting.label: FreeWheel reporting
steps: Build a report on blocked or rejected bid activity broken out by advertiser domain or category, if FreeWheel exposes it for the publisher's inventory.
Note which blocklist entries are still rejecting bid volume and which entries match no recent activity, which would point to entries that may be safe to retire.
Record whether the reporting supports keeping each entry or whether some entries block demand that no longer appears.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records, for each allowlist and blocklist entry, whether a reason, an owner, and a review date are on file, and when the lists were last reviewed as a whole. Lists where every entry has a recorded reason and a review cadence pass. Lists with entries that carry no reason, no owner, or no review since the inventory was first set up are a fail recorded with the specific entries and lists affected.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record each allowlist and blocklist entry and whether it has a reason, an owner, and a review date above to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that the buyer and advertiser allowlists and blocklists on {{site_name}}'s FreeWheel inventory are not maintained on purpose because entries stand without a recorded reason, owner, or review date. Lists set once and left in place drift away from current intent, so a blocklist can keep out demand that is no longer a concern and an allowlist can keep admitting buyers the publisher would now exclude. {{voice_recommend}} attaching a reason and a review date to every allowlist and blocklist entry, then scheduling a periodic pass that retires the entries that no longer apply. After the review, the publisher can name why each control is in force and which demand it is accepting.

audit_checks.title: Blocks are broad enough to suppress legitimate demand and fill
audit_checks.impact_rationale: A blocklist that reaches wider than the publisher intended cuts off demand that would have cleared, which lowers fill and the revenue that came with it. Category-level and buyer-level blocks are especially easy to over-apply because one entry can exclude many advertisers at once, so a single broad block can cost more fill than the narrow problem it was meant to solve.

educate.base:
Blocks protect a publisher's video and Connected TV (CTV) inventory by keeping out demand the publisher does not want, but a block is a blunt instrument when it reaches wider than intended. A blocked advertiser domain excludes one advertiser. A blocked category excludes every advertiser that falls under it, which can be a large group. A blocked buyer or demand path can exclude all the advertisers transacting through it, including ones the publisher would have welcomed. The wider the block, the more legitimate demand it can take out alongside the demand it was meant to stop.

The cost shows up as lost fill. When a bid is rejected because it matched a block, that impression has to find demand elsewhere or go unsold, and if the block was broader than the actual concern, the publisher gave up revenue it did not need to. A category block added to keep out one objectionable advertiser can reject the whole category, and a buyer-level block added after a single bad creative can reject every advertiser on that path. The narrow problem gets solved, but the wide block keeps charging fill against the publisher long after.

FreeWheel and the standards behind programmatic blocking support precision, so a publisher can usually block a specific advertiser domain rather than a whole content category, or address a creative concern at the creative or advertiser level rather than cutting off an entire buyer. The audit question is whether the blocks in force are scoped to the actual concern or whether broad blocks are doing work that narrow ones could do without the collateral loss of demand.

A few signs point to blocks that reach too broad:
  • Category-level blocks are in force where a specific advertiser domain block would address the concern
  • A whole buyer or demand path is blocked because of a problem traced to one advertiser or creative
  • Fill rate is lower on inventory carrying heavy blocks than on comparable inventory with lighter blocks, with no other cause
You may want to confirm that each block is scoped to the concern that prompted it. One approach is to compare fill on heavily blocked inventory against comparable inventory, then narrow any block that is costing more demand than the problem it addresses warrants.

site_type_overlays.publishing: On a streaming or broadcaster property, a category block added to satisfy an editorial standard can exclude far more advertisers than the standard requires, for example blocking an entire category to keep out a single advertiser within it. You may want to replace broad category blocks with specific advertiser domain blocks where the concern is one named advertiser rather than the category as a whole.
site_type_overlays.ecommerce: On a commerce-content video property, a competitor block meant to protect one merchandising relationship can exclude a whole category of retailers and the demand they bring. You may want to scope the block to the specific competing advertisers rather than the category, so the inventory keeps the unrelated demand that the broad block would otherwise reject.

educate.learn_more_note: FreeWheel's detailed configuration documentation for the scope of advertiser, category, and buyer blocks is in the partner portal (login required).

investigate.intro: Review the scope of each block in force on the publisher's FreeWheel inventory, and read fill rate on heavily blocked inventory against comparable inventory with lighter blocks.

freewheel_ui.label: FreeWheel console
steps: List the blocks in force on the publisher's inventory and record the scope of each, whether it targets a specific advertiser domain, a content category, or a whole buyer or demand path.
For each category-level or buyer-level block, note the concern that prompted it and whether a narrower block would have addressed that concern.
Record any block whose scope is wider than the problem it was meant to solve, along with the inventory it applies to.

freewheel_reporting.label: FreeWheel reporting
steps: Build a report with fill rate and revenue broken out by the inventory the blocks apply to.
Compare fill on inventory carrying heavy or broad blocks against comparable inventory with lighter blocks to see whether the broad blocks are depressing fill.
Note any case where fill is materially lower on the blocked inventory with no other cause, which points to a block that is suppressing legitimate demand.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records the scope of each block, whether it targets a specific advertiser, a category, or a whole buyer or demand path, alongside the concern that prompted it and the fill rate on the affected inventory. Blocks scoped to the actual concern, with fill on blocked inventory in line with comparable inventory, pass. Broad category-level or buyer-level blocks applied to a narrow concern, or blocks coinciding with lower fill that has no other cause, are a fail recorded with the blocks and inventory affected.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record the scope of each block and the fill rate on the inventory it applies to above to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that blocks on {{site_name}}'s FreeWheel inventory reach wider than the concerns that prompted them, with category-level or buyer-level blocks standing in for what a specific advertiser block would address. A block that reaches too wide rejects legitimate demand alongside the demand it was meant to stop, which lowers fill and the revenue that came with it. {{voice_recommend}} narrowing each broad block to the specific advertiser or creative that prompted it, then comparing fill on the affected inventory against comparable inventory to confirm the narrower scope recovers the demand the broad block was suppressing. After the change, recheck fill and revenue on the inventory to confirm the blocks protect it without cutting off demand the publisher would accept.
